message:
fix(history+evaluator+main): cross-session graduation, inline_params SQL fix, unified SIGINT registry, reentrant memory test lock

Four interlocking fixes that all touch hot/shutdown paths and share the
test-coordination lock change.

## Cross-session graduated-response wiring (`git_safety_guard-n9j1`)

`history_soft_block` / `history_hard_block` / `history_window`
config fields were parsed and merged but never consulted by
`determine_graduated_response`. For shell hooks (one process per
`Bash` call) the in-process `session_count` never grows past 1, so
Standard/Lenient modes never escalated across invocations — meaning
the cross-session graduation feature documented in the README was
silently a no-op for the hook hot path.

New API:

- `EvaluationResult::apply_graduation_with_history_count(history_count,
  config)` accepts an optional cross-session count alongside the
  existing in-process `session_count`. Standard/Lenient mode escalates
  to SoftBlock / HardBlock based on whichever signal — session or
  history — is stronger.
- `apply_graduation_with_history_db(history_db, command_hash, config)`
  is a convenience that queries
  `HistoryDb::count_command_blocks_in_window(command_hash,
  history_window_duration)` and feeds the result to the new method.
- `determine_graduated_response_with_history(session_count,
  history_count, severity, config)` is the underlying decision
  function; the legacy `determine_graduated_response` now forwards to
  it with `history_count = None`.

Hot path stays fail-open: any history-DB error (locked, schema
mismatch, missing) makes the graduation call fall back to session-only
behavior. The user never sees a hard error from a stale history
database; they just don't get cross-session escalation that call.

`ResponseConfig::parse_history_window` accepts `s` / `m` / `h` / `d`
suffixes; `history_window_duration` returns the parsed
`chrono::Duration`. 7 new tests cover the legacy-signature
equivalence, unit-parsing edge cases, and the escalation matrix.

## History `inline_params` SQL substitution corruption fix (`tovy`)

`history/schema.rs::inline_params` substituted `?N` placeholders via
reverse-order `String::replace`. Reversal solved the `?10`-vs-`?1`
ambiguity but did NOT prevent substituted-value-collides-with-earlier-
placeholder corruption: if `params[4]` was the literal text `?1`,
replacing `?5` first injected `'?1'` into the SQL, and the subsequent
pass would re-substitute it into the value of `?1`.

Replaced with a single-pass left-to-right walk over `chars()`:

- Recognizes `?N` only outside single-quoted string literals (SQLite's
  `''` doubled-quote escape is handled inline).
- Parses the full digit run after `?` so `?10` and `?100` are not
  prematurely split.
- Writes substituted values to the output without rescanning, so a
  user-supplied string containing `?1` cannot trigger a second pass.
- Uses `chars()` rather than byte indexing so non-ASCII characters in
  SQL templates (em-dash in string literal, non-ASCII column comment)
  aren't split mid-codepoint.

7 new tests including the exact regression case (`params[1] = "?1"`),
SQLite doubled-quote escape handling, and `?10`-before-`?1` ordering.

## Unified SIGINT shutdown registry (`i5gd`)

`main.rs` previously registered an ad-hoc ctrlc handler that flushed
only the `HistoryWriter`. Future stores adding cross-call buffered
state (pending exceptions, allowlists, telemetry) would either be
silently lost on SIGINT or require ad-hoc additions to the signal
handler.

Refactored to a process-wide `SHUTDOWN_ACTIONS` registry:

- `static SHUTDOWN_ACTIONS: OnceLock<Mutex<Vec<ShutdownAction>>>` holds
  the boxed flush closures.
- `register_shutdown_action(F)` appends a closure; called by each
  subsystem at startup. `install_history_shutdown_handler` now calls
  this with the history-flush closure rather than installing its own
  ctrlc handler.
- `install_signal_shutdown_handler` registers exactly one ctrlc
  handler (idempotent: duplicate `set_handler` calls are harmless),
  which iterates the registry in registration order, catches per-
  action panics so one buggy flush doesn't skip the rest, and exits
  130 (canonical SIGINT status, 128 + SIGINT(2)).
- Lock-poisoning recovery: a previous panic mid-action shouldn't
  prevent subsequent shutdown calls from flushing remaining stores,
  so `run_shutdown_actions` falls back to `into_inner()` on a
  poisoned lock.

Future stores plug in by calling `register_shutdown_action(...)`. Two
new tests verify the registry runs all registered actions and
tolerates a panicking action.

## Reentrant memory test lock (memory_tests.rs)

The `MEMORY_TEST_LOCK` mutex was held across the full body of each
memory test, which meant a test that internally invoked another
`memory_*` helper would deadlock. Switched to a thread-local
"already-holding" flag with a custom `MemoryTestLockGuard` Drop:

- First call into `memory_test_guard()` on a thread acquires the
  process-wide mutex and sets the thread-local flag.
- Re-entrant calls on the same thread return a guard with `None`
  inside, which never re-acquires the mutex — but Drop only clears
  the thread-local on the original holder.
- `MutexGuard::unwrap_or_else(PoisonError::into_inner)` recovery is
  preserved.

Tests that previously didn't call `memory_test_guard()`
(`memory_hook_input_parsing`, `memory_pattern_evaluation`,
`memory_heredoc_extraction`, `memory_extractors`) now do, since the
new reentrancy support makes the call free when the lock is already
held by the calling thread.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
